title: The whole thing, or some of it
rule: Objects: kirjan (the whole book) vs. kirjaa (at a book, still going)
summary: Finnish makes you say whether you finished it. 'Mä luen kirjan' means you'll read it to the end; 'Mä luen kirjaa' means you're in the middle of one. Same verb, same word - the ending carries the difference, and there's no English equivalent to lean on. The -n object is the whole, completed thing; the partitive (-a/-ä, -ta/-tä) is a part of it, or an action still running. This one ending decides how half your sentences are read, which is why it's worth drilling on its own.

items.prompt: Say you're in the MIDDLE of it, not finishing it
items.from_en: I'll read the book (cover to cover).
items.note: kirjan → kirjaa. The -n says you get to the end; the doubled -aa says you're somewhere inside it. Nothing else in the sentence changes.

items.prompt: Say you're eating AT it, not finishing it
items.from_en: I'll eat the apple (all of it).
items.note: omenan → omenaa. Stems ending in -a just double it: omena + a = omenaa.

items.from_en: I'll watch the film (to the end).
items.note: leffan → leffaa. 'Katon' is the spoken form of 'katson' - it stays put; only the object moves.

items.prompt: Say you're drinking one, not downing it
items.from_en: I'll have a beer (the whole glass).
items.note: Here the partitive is -ta, not -a: olut → olutta. Words ending in a consonant take the -ta/-tä form. This is the one you have to learn per word.

items.prompt: Say you're buying some, not one specific carton
items.note: maitoa in writing, maitoo in speech - spoken Finnish lengthens the vowel instead of saying -oa. Milk is a mass, so the partitive is the everyday form.

items.prompt: Say you're cooking, not that you'll finish a dish
items.note: Watch the k: ruoka loses it in ruoan (weak grade) but keeps it in ruokaa (strong). The partitive is built on the strong stem - that's consonant gradation, drilled properly in its own set.

items.prompt: Say you're learning it, not that you'll master it
items.from_en: I'll learn the song (all of it).
items.note: laulun → laulua. A skill in progress is almost always partitive - which is why 'mä opettelen suomee' is partitive too, never 'suomen'.

items.prompt: Say you're writing it right now
items.from_en: I'll write the message (and send it).
items.note: viestiä in writing, viestii in speech. Words in -i do the same lengthening as kahvi → kahvii.

items.prompt: Say you're washing it, not that you'll finish
items.note: autoa → autoo, the same -oa lengthening as maitoa → maitoo.

items.prompt: Now say you're WAITING for it instead
items.note: Same object, different verb, and the ending has to move: nähdä takes bussin, but odottaa only ever takes the partitive. 'Mä odotan bussin' isn't a shade of meaning - it's simply wrong. Waiting has no finish line built into it.

items.prompt: Now say you LOVE them - same rule
items.note: nähdä takes sut, rakastaa takes sua (sinua in writing). Never 'rakastan sun' - that's the one that makes a Finn wince.

items.prompt: Now say you'll HELP them - partitive again
items.note: tuntea takes sut, auttaa takes sua. Worth memorising the four partitive verbs together - odottaa, rakastaa, auttaa, harrastaa - because they come up constantly and the rule has no logic to reconstruct from.
